name: Ibon de Plan (Basa de la Mora) from San Juan de Plan
name_original: Ibón de Plan (Basa de la Mora) desde San Juan de Plan
region_intro: Hiking trails around San Juan De Plan are set within the Aragonese Pyrenees, at an altitude of 1,120 meters, serving as a gateway to the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park. The region is characterized by towering peaks ranging between 2,000 and 3,000 meters, extensive pine and fir forests, and high alpine meadows. Numerous glacial lakes, known as ibones, are prominent natural features, often forming the focal point of many routes. The landscape also features the Cinqueta River, offering diverse exploration options. Best hiking trails around San Juan De Plan The most popular hiking route is Ibon de Plan (Basa de la Mora) from San Juan de Plan, a 10.2 miles (16.5 km) trail that takes 7 hours 20 minutes to complete. This difficult route leads to a beautiful mountain lake. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ibón del Sen desde San Juan de Plan — Parque Natural Posets-Maladeta, a difficult 10.2 miles (16.5 km) path. This route offers demanding terrain within the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park, culminating at the Ibón del Sen. Local hikers also love the Ibones de Barbarisa from puerto de Sahún, a 7.6 miles (12.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain to a series of glacial lakes, often completed in about 5 hours 15 minutes. Hiking around San Juan De Plan is defined by majestic mountains, lush forests, and pristine glacial lakes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate walks to challenging high-mountain ascents. The routes in San Juan De Plan are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 380 reviews. More than 4000 hikers have used komoot to explore San Juan De Plan's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of hiking difficulty can I expect around San Juan De Plan?
A: The San Juan De Plan area offers a diverse range of hiking difficulties. You'll find options from moderate walks through valleys and forests to challenging high-mountain ascents. Out of 18 available routes, 2 are considered easy, 7 are moderate, and 9 are difficult, catering to various fitness levels.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking trails suitable for children in San Juan De Plan?
A: Yes, alongside the more strenuous climbs, there are gentler paths through terraces and forests that are ideal for leisurely strolls with families. These routes allow for connecting with nature without demanding high levels of fitness. While specific easy routes aren't detailed here, the region offers options for all ages.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in San Juan De Plan?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on trails in the Aragonese Pyrenees, but it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially within protected areas like the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park, to protect wildlife and livestock. Always check specific park regulations before heading out.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available in the San Juan De Plan area?
A: Yes, there are circular options. For example, you can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1194711405/lakes-of-barbarisa-and-foradet-waterfall-circular-from-collado-de-sahun" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lakes of Barbarisa and Foradet Waterfall — circular from Collado de Sahún&lt;/a&gt;. This route offers a rewarding experience through mountainous terrain, including a beautiful waterfall, and brings you back to your starting point.

Q: Are there any notable waterfalls to see while hiking near San Juan De Plan?
A: The landscape around San Juan De Plan is dotted with impressive waterfalls. A prominent one is the Foradet Waterfall, which can be included in circular routes to the Ibones de Barbarisa. You can also find the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4809461" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorgas de Calantes Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, offering another scenic natural feature to explore.

Q: What are some of the most beautiful mountain lakes (ibones) I can hike to?
A: The region is famous for its pristine glacial lakes, known as ibones. Two highly recommended destinations are &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931389388/ibon-de-plan-basa-de-la-mora-from-san-juan-de-plan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón de Plan (Basa de la Mora)&lt;/a&gt;, a popular and challenging hike leading to a stunning lake, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1387351908/ibon-del-sen-desde-san-juan-de-plan-parque-natural-posets-maladeta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibón del Sen&lt;/a&gt;, another rewarding but demanding hike within the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e946996622/ibones-de-barbarisa-from-puerto-de-sahun" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibones de Barbarisa&lt;/a&gt; also offer a series of beautiful mountain lakes.

Q: Where can I find parking facilities for trailheads in San Juan De Plan?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not listed, San Juan De Plan, as a gateway to the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park, typically has designated parking areas near popular starting points for hikes. It's advisable to arrive early during peak season to secure a spot, especially for well-known routes like those leading to the ibones.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trailheads from San Juan De Plan?
A: Public transport options directly to remote trailheads from San Juan De Plan can be limited. It's often recommended to use a personal vehicle to access many of the more distant starting points. However, local bus services might connect San Juan De Plan with larger towns, from which further arrangements may be needed.

Q: Do I need a permit to hike in Posets-Maladeta Natural Park?
A: Generally, day hiking in the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park does not require a specific permit. However, certain activities like camping, climbing, or accessing specific restricted areas might require authorization. It's always best to check the official Posets-Maladeta Natural Park website for the most current regulations before your visit.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in San Juan De Plan?
A: The best time for hiking in San Juan De Plan is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally more stable, and the high mountain passes are usually free of snow. Summer offers lush alpine meadows, while autumn brings vibrant fall colors. Always check local weather forecasts, especially for high-altitude routes.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in San Juan De Plan?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 380 reviews. Hikers often praise the majestic mountain scenery, the pristine glacial lakes (ibones), and the diverse terrain that ranges from lush forests to high alpine meadows. The sense of immersion in nature and the well-maintained trails are frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical points of interest in San Juan De Plan itself?
A: Yes, beyond its natural grandeur, San Juan De Plan offers cultural charm. The village maintains its traditional 'chistabina' architecture, featuring stone houses with thick walls, slate roofs, and conical chimneys. You can also visit the Posets-Maladeta Natural Park Interpretation Center, located within the village, for insights into the park's biological processes and landscapes.

Q: Can I find opportunities for wild swimming or water sports near San Juan De Plan?
A: The Cinqueta River, which flows through the region, provides opportunities for exhilarating water sports like canyoning and kayaking, especially during spring and summer when water levels are higher. While specific wild swimming spots are not officially designated, many of the glacial lakes (ibones) offer tranquil resting spots where some may choose to take a refreshing dip, though water temperatures are often very cold.
tips: The climb up the ravine is steep and you need a steady foot. It can be avoided by reaching the Lavasar Refuge by car along a forest track in good condition.

The Plan or Basa de la Mora lake is located at an altitude of 1,910 meters and fills up after the thaw each winter.
The name of Basa de la Mora (or "Basa la Mora" in Aragonese) comes from a local folktale according to which a Moorish princess drowned in this lake while trying to escape Christian troops. According to this legend, during the night of San Juan, people who wash their faces with the lake water can see the princess dancing on the surface.

This unguarded refuge is very close to Ibón de Plan and provides a 360 degree panoramic view. On one side are the sharp ridges of the mountains that surround the glacial cirque, and on the other side you can see, in the distance, the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park.
